title: China's Chip Surge and the Infrastructure Wars
subhead: US export controls squeeze Hua Hong while Huawei's Ascend chips ride DeepSeek's breakthrough — but Beijing's threat to Europe over telecoms bans and Africa's mineral sovereignty push reveal a deeper contest for the infrastructure of power.

sections.heading: US Chokes Hua Hong's 7nm Ambitions
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department sent "is-informed" letters to multiple American chip equipment makers last week ordering them to halt shipments to Hua Hong's contract manufacturing unit, Huali Microelectronics. The targeted facilities were preparing a 7-nanometer process that would bring China's second-largest foundry closer to producing chips suitable for modern AI workloads.

The move extends Washington's export control dragnet beyond SMIC, China's leading chipmaker, to the foundry that handles most of the country's mature-node production. Hua Hong specializes in legacy process nodes used in automotive, industrial, and consumer electronics — but its push toward 7nm represented a deliberate attempt to climb into the advanced-logic arena now dominated by TSMC and Samsung.

The letters target specific Hua Hong facilities, suggesting US intelligence identified which fabs were being retooled for finer geometries. Lam Research, Applied Materials, and KLA — the Silicon Valley toolmaking trio — are believed to have received the notices. The restrictions could cost US equipment suppliers billions in sales, especially if Hua Hong's expansion plans stall permanently. But the Commerce Department's Bureau of Industry and Security has made clear that revenue concerns won't slow the chokehold: export controls are now Washington's primary lever in the semiconductor race, and Beijing's attempts to advance beyond legacy nodes will face American friction at every turn.
sections.whyItMatters: Hua Hong's 7nm push was China's backup plan for domestic advanced chips. US export controls have now locked in a two-tier world: SMIC constrained at the frontier, Hua Hong blocked from graduating beyond mature nodes, and American toolmakers absorbing the revenue hit as the price of technological containment.

sections.heading: DeepSeek V4 Ignites Chinese Demand for Huawei's Ascend Chips
sections.body:
DeepSeek's release of its V4 artificial intelligence model — optimized specifically for Huawei's Ascend 950 chip series — has triggered a scramble among China's technology giants to secure domestic AI hardware. ByteDance, Tencent, Alibaba, and cloud computing providers have rushed to place orders for Huawei's in-house semiconductors, marking what Reuters describes as a potential turning point for the Shenzhen-based company.

DeepSeek V4 opens a window for Huawei to prove its Ascend chips can compete with NVIDIA in the AI training and inference market that US export controls have made inaccessible. The model's optimization for Ascend architecture provides Chinese firms with a viable path to advanced AI workloads without relying on smuggled or restricted NVIDIA hardware. Huawei expects to ship around 750,000 units of the 950PR this year, with full-scale shipments ramping through the second half of 2026.

Supply remains tight. Production of domestic chips continues to be constrained by US export controls on advanced manufacturing tools — the same controls now squeezing Hua Hong. But the DeepSeek V4 launch demonstrates that software-hardware integration can partially compensate for silicon disadvantages: if Chinese AI models are built to run efficiently on Chinese chips, the performance gap matters less. This is Beijing's semiconductor strategy in miniature: substitute quantity and software optimization for the lithographic advantage Washington has denied them.
sections.whyItMatters: Huawei's Ascend chips have struggled to win large orders from China's tech sector — until now. DeepSeek V4 creates a domestic validation case that transforms Ascend from a forced alternative into a legitimate competitor. The question is whether Huawei can scale production fast enough to meet demand before US export controls strangle the tool supply needed to manufacture the chips.

sections.heading: Beijing Threatens Europe With Broad Retaliation Over Telecoms Ban
sections.body:
China's Ministry of Commerce has formally submitted a 30-page document to the European Commission warning of reciprocal measures if the EU's draft Cybersecurity Act leads to the exclusion of Huawei and ZTE from European networks. The threat invokes China's Foreign Trade Law and State Council Supply Chain Security Regulations — legal frameworks that allow Beijing to restrict trade, investigate foreign entities, and impose reciprocal bans.

The EU's proposed law would transform the existing 5G toolbox 'recommendation' into a binding obligation, requiring member states to remove equipment from designated high-risk vendors within three years. The law doesn't name Huawei or ZTE explicitly, but EU Tech Commissioner Henna Virkkunen's framing makes the intent clear: the Commission wants 'the means to better protect our critical supply chains.' Strand Consult data puts Chinese vendors' share of European 5G infrastructure at 33-40%. A full removal would be the largest forced telecoms infrastructure replacement in European history.

Beijing's threat is credible because it has precedent. When Sweden banned Chinese vendors from its 5G networks in 2020, Ericsson's China revenues fell 46% the following year and never recovered. Nokia's China market share has collapsed from roughly €2.5 billion in 2018 to €913 million last year. The asymmetry is pointed: China has already been restricting the very European companies that would benefit from a Huawei ban, while warning that formal exclusion will trigger broader retaliation across 18 critical sectors — energy, transport, information technology, and beyond.

Germany faces the hardest choice. Huawei equipment covers approximately 60% of German 5G sites. The practical reality of a three-year EU-wide rip-and-replace is, as industry analysts note, 'ambitious and compliance is not certain.' But Berlin must also consider what Beijing's retaliation would mean for German automakers with €90 billion in annual China exports, not to mention the broader dependency of European industry on Chinese supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is caught between Washington's pressure to expel Huawei and Beijing's threat of asymmetric economic warfare. China has already weaponized market access against Nokia and Ericsson — the very companies Europe would need to replace Chinese kit. The Cybersecurity Act forces Brussels to choose: accept permanent Chinese telecoms dependency, or risk coordinated retaliation across the sectors where European industry depends on Chinese manufacturing and markets.

sections.heading: Europe's AI Act Negotiations Collapse Under Regulatory Fatigue
sections.body:
EU member states and European Parliament lawmakers failed to reach a deal on watered-down artificial intelligence rules after 12 hours of negotiations on Tuesday, pushing talks into next month. The collapse reflects growing tension between Brussels' regulatory ambitions and the practical concerns of member states worried about competitive disadvantage.

The AI Act's compliance costs will hit European startups hardest — exactly the companies that could compete with American and Chinese AI firms. While Brussels debates 'catastrophic risk' frameworks and transparency requirements, Washington and Beijing are racing ahead with industrial policy that treats AI development as a national security imperative, not a regulatory compliance problem.

California and New York have now passed their own AI governance laws — SB 53 and the RAISE Act — establishing transparency requirements and catastrophic risk reporting for foundation model developers. The US state-level approach, harmonized rapidly through the 'Sacramento Effect,' creates a patchwork that American companies can navigate while European counterparts face the prospect of the world's most prescriptive AI regulation. The timing matters: DeepSeek's V4 breakthrough shows Chinese AI models are reaching competitive parity with Western offerings precisely as European policymakers struggle to agree on what 'risk' even means in the AI context.
sections.whyItMatters: Every month Brussels spends negotiating AI rules is a month American and Chinese companies advance without equivalent constraint. The regulatory gap compounds: European startups face compliance overhead that US and Chinese competitors don't, while China's domestic AI ecosystem — powered by Huawei chips and DeepSeek models — operates in a completely different enforcement environment.

sections.heading: NSA's GrassMarlin Tool Leaves Critical Infrastructure Vulnerable
sections.body:
CISA has warned that GrassMarlin, an operational technology network analysis tool developed and open-sourced by the NSA, contains a vulnerability that allows attackers to exfiltrate sensitive information. The flaw (CVE-2026-6807) affects all versions of the software, which went end-of-life in 2017 and will receive no patches.

GrassMarlin was designed to help critical infrastructure organizations secure their industrial control systems and SCADA networks — exactly the kind of tool that, when compromised, undermines the security posture of the facilities it was meant to protect. The vulnerability is an XML External Entity (XXE) attack vector: maliciously crafted session files can trick the tool into exfiltrating arbitrary files to external hosts. A public proof-of-concept exploit is already available on GitHub.

The incident illustrates a broader structural problem in infrastructure security: tools built by intelligence agencies for defensive purposes age into liabilities when unmaintained. GrassMarlin users — primarily critical infrastructure operators — now have to assess whether their security tool was itself an attack surface. The irony is pointed: an NSA-built defensive tool, left to rot, becomes a potential vector for the same adversaries it was designed to counter.
sections.whyItMatters: Critical infrastructure security depends on tool chains that age into vulnerabilities. GrassMarlin's XXE flaw won't be patched — CISA's advisory recommends isolation and access controls, not fixes. The incident underscores that defensive cyber tools require the same lifecycle management as offensive capabilities, and that intelligence agencies open-sourcing security software without maintenance plans create long-term risks for the operators they intended to protect.

sections.heading: Medical Device Cyberattacks Rise as Legacy Tech Creates Structural Vulnerability
sections.body:
A report from RunSafe Security finds that 24% of healthcare organizations experienced a cyberattack on a medical device in 2026, up 2 percentage points from the previous year. Of those attacked, 80% reported moderate or significant patient care impact. The most commonly affected systems: electronic health records (35%), patient monitoring devices (23%), and laboratory equipment (18%).

Three in ten healthcare organizations operate medical devices past the manufacturer's end-of-support date — creating a structural security gap that cannot be closed through procurement alone. Legacy devices cannot be patched, clinical workflows depend on them, and replacements are either unavailable or unaffordable. The result: a persistent attack surface that ransomware operators and state actors can exploit for disruption or intelligence collection.

Remote access exploitation emerged as a growing threat vector in 2026, seen in 38% of incidents — indicating attackers are adapting to the expanded remote access footprint of connected devices. Runtime protection technologies, which defend devices without requiring patches, are emerging as a compensating control, with 82% of respondents now widely deploying or piloting such solutions. But runtime protection addresses symptoms, not causes: the underlying problem is a healthcare technology stack built on devices that were never designed for the networked threat environment they now inhabit.
sections.whyItMatters: Healthcare cyber vulnerability is now a patient safety issue. Legacy devices create a persistent gap that no amount of runtime protection can fully close, and the sector's clinical and financial constraints prevent rapid modernization. The 24% attack rate will rise as adversaries — both criminal and state — recognize that medical device compromise offers both ransomware revenue and potential intelligence collection opportunities against populations of interest.

sections.heading: Africa Demands Value-Addition Over Raw Mineral Exports
sections.body:
Nigeria's Minister of Solid Minerals Development, Oladele Alake, speaking at the Kenya Mining Investment Conference, urged African nations to stop exporting raw minerals and instead build processing capacity to capture more value from critical mineral supply chains. The Africa Minerals Strategy Group, established in January 2023 by 16 member states, has now expanded to 31 countries.

Africa holds significant shares of the world's lithium, cobalt, manganese, graphite, and rare earth elements — the inputs for electric vehicles, renewable energy systems, and electronics — but captures only a fraction of the value generated from these resources. The continent loses billions annually due to limited local processing capacity and dependence on imports of finished products. Alake's argument is straightforward: raw material export is a development dead end, and only coordinated regional action can shift the bargaining power that determines who captures mineral wealth.

The African Continental Free Trade Area and regional platforms like the Minerals Strategy Group represent attempts to reverse this dynamic. But the barriers are structural: limited infrastructure, weak geological data systems, regulatory fragmentation across 54 countries, and investment environments that favor Chinese and Western firms with established processing capabilities. Africa's mineral sovereignty push will compete with China's existing processing dominance and Western efforts to 'friend-shore' supply chains away from Beijing. The continent has the resources; the question is whether it can build the political coordination and infrastructure to capture the processing step that transforms raw earth into industrial power.
sections.whyItMatters: Africa supplies the critical minerals that power the global energy transition but remains price-takers in the markets that matter. The Minerals Strategy Group's expansion to 31 countries signals a coordination attempt — but without processing infrastructure and political unity, African mineral wealth will continue to flow east and west as raw material, with the value-add accruing elsewhere. China's solar equipment export restrictions (another story today) demonstrate exactly why mineral sovereignty matters: whoever controls processing controls leverage.

bottomLine: The day's dominant tech-power shift runs through the semiconductor supply chain: US export controls on Hua Hong tighten the vise on China's foundry options, while DeepSeek V4 creates sudden domestic demand for Huawei's Ascend chips — a dual signal that Washington's containment strategy and Beijing's substitution strategy are now racing in parallel. The EU's Cybersecurity Act negotiations, China's retaliation threat, and Africa's mineral sovereignty push all flow from the same structural reality: the infrastructure of technological power — chips, telecoms, minerals — determines who gets to compete and who gets squeezed. The US has chosen to squeeze; China has chosen to substitute; Europe must now choose between Washington's pressure and Beijing's retaliation; and Africa is trying to choose for itself.
sources: Reuters, Mobile World Live, Hoodline, The Next Web, IAPP, The Register, Silicon Republic, Marketplace, South China Morning Post (via TNW), PunchNG
